docs.title: Quantum Notes
docs.text:
Personal notes while learning quantum computing — concepts, circuits, and the occasional broken intuition.

Why this exists

Writing things down forces me to actually understand them. These notes are a work in progress and may contain mistakes — if you spot one, [open an issue]

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• Qubits & Superposition — what a qubit actually is, and why it's not "0 and 1 at the same time"
  • Quantum Gates — how gates transform qubit states, with circuit diagrams

docs.title: How I'm learning
docs.text:
• Working through Qiskit tutorials and running circuits on real IBM hardware
  • Cross-referencing with Nielsen & Chuang for the math
  • Re-deriving things by hand before trusting that I understand them

Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: Quantum Gates
docs.text: Gates are how you manipulate qubit states. Unlike classical logic gates, quantum gates must be reversible — represented by unitary matrices.

docs.title: The Hadamard gate (H)
docs.text:
Puts a qubit into equal superposition:

\[ H = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \] \[ H|0\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle + |1\rangle) \]

docs.title: The CNOT gate (entanglement)
docs.text:
Flips the target qubit only if the control qubit is 1. This is how entanglement gets created between two qubits.

graph LR
    subgraph Circuit
        q0["q0: control"] --> H["H"]
        H --> CNOT["CNOT"]
        q1["q1: target"] --> CNOT
        CNOT --> out0["measure q0"]
        CNOT --> out1["measure q1"]
    end
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2, 2)
qc.h(0)        # superposition on control qubit
qc.cx(0, 1)    # entangle control and target
qc.measure([0, 1], [0, 1])

print(qc.draw())

What to notice

After this circuit, measuring q0 and q1 always gives matching results (00 or 11), never 01 or 10 — even though each individual qubit's outcome looks random. That correlation is entanglement.

docs.title: Gate cheat sheet
docs.text:
Gate Symbol Effect X Pauli-X Bit flip (classical NOT) Z Pauli-Z Phase flip H Hadamard Creates superposition CNOT Controlled-X Entangles two qubits T T-gate π/4 phase rotation

← Back to Qubits & Superposition

docs.title: The state of a qubit
docs.text:
A classical bit is 0 or 1. A qubit's state is a vector in a 2D complex space:

\[ |\psi\rangle = \alpha|0\rangle + \beta|1\rangle \]

where \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are complex numbers satisfying:

\[ |\alpha|^2 + |\beta|^2 = 1 \]

Common misconception

"Superposition" doesn't mean the qubit is secretly 0 and 1 at the same time. It means the qubit's state is a combination of both basis states, and measurement collapses it to one outcome with a probability set by \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\).

docs.title: Measurement
docs.text:
Measuring \(|\psi\rangle\) in the standard basis gives:

  • 0 with probability \(|\alpha|^2\)
  • 1 with probability \(|\beta|^2\)
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit_aer import AerSimulator

qc = QuantumCircuit(1, 1)
qc.h(0)          # put qubit into superposition
qc.measure(0, 0)

sim = AerSimulator()
result = sim.run(qc, shots=1000).result()
print(result.get_counts())
# roughly {'0': 500, '1': 500}

docs.title: Bloch sphere intuition
docs.text:
A single qubit's state can be visualized as a point on the surface of a sphere:

graph TD
    A["|0⟩ — North pole"] --- B["Superposition states — equator & elsewhere"]
    B --- C["|1⟩ — South pole"]
    B --- D["Phase determines position around the sphere"]

The poles are the classical states. Everything else on the sphere's surface is a valid superposition — phase and amplitude both matter, which is why two states can have the same measurement probabilities but behave differently in a circuit.
